answers.intake_q1: When did your back pain start?
answers.intake_q2: Can you describe where exactly your back pain is located?
answers.intake_q3: Any recent injury or trauma to your back or ribs?
answers.intake_q4: Any fever, chills, or night sweats since the pain started?
answers.intake_q5: Any shortness of breath, coughing, or chest pain along with your back pain?
answers.intake_q6: Any numbness, weakness, or tingling in your legs or feet?
answers.intake_q7: Have you noticed any swelling, bruising, or skin changes over the painful area?
answers.intake_q8: Have you tried any treatments or medications for your back pain, and did they help?
answers.intake_a8: I tried tylenol but it didn't really help much
answers.intake_q9: Any history of kidney problems, recent urinary symptoms, or abdominal pain?
answers.intake_q10: Have you noticed any recent unexplained weight loss or fatigue?

note.soap:
S: 
The patient is a 47-year-old female presenting with left-sided lower rib cage back pain that started approximately three weeks ago. She denies any recent injury or trauma, fever, chills, night sweats, respiratory symptoms, numbness, weakness, tingling, swelling, bruising, or skin changes. She has tried acetaminophen (Tylenol) without significant relief. There is no history of kidney problems, urinary symptoms, abdominal pain, unexplained weight loss, or fatigue. Patient is not pregnant.

O: 
Phone evaluation only; no physical examination or vital signs available.

A: 
The working diagnosis is mechanical back pain localized to the left lower rib cage area, likely musculoskeletal in origin given the absence of systemic symptoms or neurological deficits. 

Differential: 
1. Musculoskeletal strain or sprain 
2. Intercostal muscle strain or rib dysfunction 
3. Early costochondritis or rib inflammation 
4. Referred pain from visceral sources (less likely given no abdominal or urinary symptoms) 

P: 
- Acetaminophen 500–1000 mg orally every 6 hours as needed for pain; max 3 grams/day (adjust for liver disease).
 - Alternatives: Acetaminophen can be continued if NSAIDs are contraindicated or not tolerated. 
 - Nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs) such as ibuprofen or naproxen for pain relief and inflammation reduction. (avoid in renal disease; especially severe CKD/dialysis)
 - Medication safety considerations: Avoid NSAIDs if there is a history of gastrointestinal bleeding, renal impairment, or liver disease (none reported). Use the lowest effective dose for the shortest duration. 
 - Return precautions: Advise the patient to seek care if she develops fever, worsening pain, neurological symptoms (numbness, weakness, tingling), unexplained weight loss, or new urinary symptoms. 
 - Testing: No imaging or laboratory testing is indicated at this time given the absence of red flag symptoms.

evidence.title: NIAMS: Living With Back Pain (patient-friendly NIH resource)
evidence.snippet: Acute back pain happens suddenly and usually lasts a few days to a few weeks. Back pain that lasts longer than 12 weeks is called chronic back pain.   Who Gets Back Pain? Image Anyone can have back pain. You may be more likely to have back pain because of the following: Age: Back pain is more common as you age. Fitness level: Back pain is more common if…

evidence.title: Back Injuries | MedlinePlus
evidence.snippet: extending from your neck to your pelvis. Back injuries can result from sports injuries, work around the house or in the garden, or a sudden jolt such as a car accident. evidence.title: AAFP Choosing Wisely: Imaging for Low Back Pain (Red Flags)
evidence.snippet: Information Low back pain is one of the most common reasons for an outpatient visit.
